Embedding source maps directly into code is common in development or build artifacts, especially for debugging minified code.\n\n- **Malware score:** All reports assign a malware score of 0, which is appropriate. There is no evidence of malicious code, such as network activity, code injection, or obfuscated malicious logic.\n\n- **Security risk scores:** The scores are very low (mostly 0 or 0.1-0.2), reflecting the benign nature of the code, with slight increases due to the unusual embedding of source maps.\n\n- **Potential assumptions:** Some reports assume that embedded source maps could be used maliciously. While embedding source maps can obscure code, it is a standard practice in certain build processes and not inherently malicious.\n\n### Final recommendations:\n\n- **Malware score:** Confirmed as 0 across all reports, consistent with the analysis.\n- **Obfuscated score:** Correctly 0; the code is straightforward.\n- **Risk score:** Slightly elevated (up to 0.2) due to inline source map embedding, which is reasonable but not indicative of malicious intent.\n\n### My overall estimate:\n- **Malware:** 0\n- **Obfuscated:** 0\n- **Risk:** 0.1 to 0.2 (consistent with the reports)\n\n**Conclusion:** The reports are accurate. The code is benign, and the embedded source map, while unusual, is standard in build artifacts.
